1.matchText: Scythians had Greek colonies on the Black Sea coast
1.blurb: The Scythians built their wealth on trade with Greek colonies along the Black Sea — exporting grain, hides, and slaves in exchange for wine, olive oil, and luxury metalwork. This was a fundamentally different relationship with the settled world than the Xiongnu had with China. The Scythians traded as partners; the Xiongnu demanded tribute.

The Zhou dynasty's feudal system gave local lords enormous autonomy, which is why the northern states of Zhao, Yan, and Qin were building their own defensive walls independently. The collapse of Zhou central authority during the Spring and Autumn period created the fragmented Chinese frontier that the Xiongnu's predecessors exploited.

1.matchText: created the first unified Chinese empire
1.blurb: Qin Shi Huang conquered the last rival kingdoms in 221 BCE and immediately turned to the northern frontier. His general Meng Tian drove the Xiongnu from the Ordos and connected existing walls into the first Great Wall — the Qin answer to the nomadic threat was characteristically ambitious and characteristically brutal.

1.matchText: Maurya Empire at its height under Ashoka
1.blurb: While the Xiongnu were organizing on the Mongolian steppe, Ashoka was ruling an Indian empire stretching from Afghanistan to Bengal and carving his edicts of nonviolence into rock pillars. The Maurya and Xiongnu empires were exact contemporaries and exact opposites — one built on dharma, the other on arrows.
As the Xiongnu coalesced on the eastern steppe, the Roman Republic was consolidating Italy — both building the power that would define their corner of the world, neither aware the other existed.
India's Maurya Empire was at its height under Ashoka while the steppe pieces fell into place for Modu's nomadic empire — two utterly different answers to the problem of organizing a continent.
China was in the final bloody phase of the Warring States — the chaos that would produce the Qin and then the Han, the settled superpower the Xiongnu were about to rival.
At the western edge of the known world, Alexander's successor kingdoms were carving up his empire — the Hellenistic age, contemporary with the first stirrings of the Xiongnu.

2.matchText: Scythians had proved that nomads could humiliate empires
2.blurb: The Scythians humiliated Darius I of Persia in 513 BCE by refusing to fight — burning the grass, poisoning the wells, and retreating endlessly until the Persian army nearly starved. It proved that nomads could defeat settled empires, but the Scythians never tried to build a political structure to match. That was Modu's innovation.

2.matchText: Qin dynasty in China was collapsing
2.blurb: The Qin dynasty lasted only fifteen years. Qin Shi Huang died in 210 BCE, and within months the empire collapsed into civil war as peasant rebels, regional warlords, and former aristocrats fought over the wreckage. Modu's seizure of power in 209 BCE was perfectly timed — China was too busy destroying itself to notice a steppe empire forming on its doorstep.

2.matchText: Hannibal had crossed the Alps in 218 BCE
2.blurb: Hannibal's crossing of the Alps and his rampage through Italy nearly destroyed Rome. While Modu was building a steppe empire in Mongolia, Carthage was fighting for Mediterranean supremacy 5,000 miles away. Both were challenging the dominant power in their region at exactly the same moment — and neither knew the other existed.
Modu seized power in 209 BCE — the very year the Qin dynasty collapsed into the civil war that birthed the Han. Steppe and sown were convulsing in the same instant.
Out of the Qin collapse rose the Han — the empire that would spend the next century locked in a tribute-and-war relationship with Modu's Xiongnu.
While China fought for survival against itself, Rome fought for survival against Hannibal — and neither noticed the empire forming on the Mongolian grass.
Rome and Carthage were locked in the war that would decide the western Mediterranean, oblivious to the nomadic superpower rising at the other end of Eurasia.

3.blurb: Rome razed Carthage in 146 BCE with legendary thoroughness — destroying the city, salting the earth (probably legendary), and enslaving the survivors. While China was paying silk tribute to keep the Xiongnu at bay, Rome was eliminating its greatest rival by total annihilation. Two very different approaches to existential threats.

3.matchText: Persians had been humiliated by the Scythians
3.blurb: Darius I's invasion of Scythian territory around 513 BCE ended in humiliation — the Scythians refused to fight, retreated endlessly, and nearly starved the Persian army. But the Persians never paid systematic tribute the way Han China paid the Xiongnu. China's heqin system was unique in the ancient world: the richest civilization on earth formally buying peace from nomads it called barbarians.
As Han China bought peace from the Xiongnu with the heqin marriages, Rome was erasing Carthage from the earth (146 BCE) — two superpowers, two opposite ways of handling a rival.
India's Maurya Empire was fading just as the heqin system strained — the great classical states all entering transitions at once.

4.matchText: destruction of Corinth and Carthage in 146 BCE
4.blurb: Rome destroyed Corinth and Carthage in the same year, 146 BCE, effectively ending Greek and Punic independence in a single season. While Emperor Wu was planning his war against the Xiongnu, the Mediterranean world was being forcibly unified under Roman power — two empires at opposite ends of Eurasia both extending their reach at the same moment.

4.matchText: Roman Republic's expansion across the Mediterranean
4.blurb: While Emperor Wu was sending cavalry across the Gobi Desert to break the Xiongnu, the Roman Republic was tearing itself apart in civil wars — Marius vs. Sulla, Pompey vs. Caesar. Both empires were at peak military ambition, expanding in opposite directions along routes that would eventually become the Silk Road.

Emperor Wu's cavalry wars against the Xiongnu run parallel to the late Roman Republic's Mediterranean conquests — Rome expanding by infantry and engineering, China by cavalry and logistics, both unknowingly building toward the Silk Road.

5.matchText: Augustus established the Principate in 27 BCE
5.blurb: Augustus transformed Rome from a dying republic into an empire that would last five centuries. At the same moment, the Xiongnu were splitting into rival factions and Huhanye was bowing to the Han emperor. Both ends of Eurasia were reorganizing themselves around single-ruler imperial systems — and both would eventually face the same problem of frontier nomads they couldn't permanently defeat.
The Xiongnu fractured during the span from the late Roman Republic to Augustus's Principate — the two great empires at either end of Eurasia near their peaks, both fighting frontier nomads.
Rome reached its greatest extent under Trajan while the Northern Xiongnu were being driven west — the Silk Road linking two empires that never grasped each other's scale.

6.blurb: The Scythians had dominated the Pontic steppe for five centuries before being compressed into Crimea by the Sarmatians. When the Huns arrived in the same grasslands around 370 CE, they found a landscape that had been shaped by centuries of nomadic power — and they used it the same way, as a base for raiding the settled empires to the south.

6.matchText: worst Roman military defeat since Cannae
6.blurb: Adrianople in 378 CE was a catastrophe on the scale of Cannae — a Roman emperor killed, an army destroyed, and the military prestige of Rome shattered. The chain of causation ran directly from the Hunnic invasion of the Pontic steppe to the Gothic refugees to the disaster at Adrianople. The Huns hadn't even fought Rome yet, and they'd already destabilized the empire.
The Hunnic arrival in Europe (~370 CE) fell in Rome's twilight, with Sassanid Persia as Rome's great eastern rival — the steppe sending shockwaves into a fragmenting classical world.
In China the same steppe pressure had already shattered the north into 'barbarian' kingdoms — including the Xiongnu-descended Han Zhao — while Chinese dynasties clung to the south.

7.matchText: the Persians on the eastern frontier
7.blurb: The Sassanid Persian Empire was Rome's great eastern rival for four centuries, and in 441 CE their war with Constantinople gave Attila his opening to ravage the Balkans. The Persians and the Huns were both extracting wealth from the Roman Empire simultaneously — from opposite directions.
Attila's campaigns fell in the last decades of the Western Roman Empire, which would formally end just 23 years after his death.
While the West collapsed, the Eastern Roman Empire thrived behind Constantinople's Theodosian Walls — the half of Rome that the Huns could not crack.
In Mesoamerica the great city of Teotihuacan was at its height — the classical world fragmenting under steppe pressure in the Old World while a metropolis peaked, unconnected, in the New.

In the very decade Attila devastated Gaul, the Gupta emperor Skandagupta was repulsing the first Hephthalite incursion on India. Two empires under steppe pressure at once — the West fell, India held a generation longer.

8.blurb: The Jurchens that this paragraph rattles off as just one more name on the list of northern nomadic threats are the subject of an entire chapter in the Sui-Tang-Song narrative. In 1127 they smashed the Northern Song, captured two emperors, and pushed the Chinese court below the Yangtze — the same Xiongnu-template playbook of mounted archery, tribute extortion, and eventual partial absorption into Chinese culture, replayed a thousand years later with consequences that defined the next two centuries of East Asian history.

8.matchText: Gokturks were about to displace them in turn
8.blurb: The Gokturks that this final cross-civ check name-checks are the next great steppe power in the Xiongnu lineage — the first people in history to call themselves 'Turk,' founders in 552 of a khaganate that briefly ran the entire Eurasian steppe from Manchuria to the Black Sea. The Gokturk Khaganate narrative opens here, on the same Mongolian grassland the Xiongnu had ruled seven centuries earlier, with the same decimal military organization and the same dual-wing structure that Modu had pioneered.

8.matchText: fall in 476 CE had many causes
8.blurb: The Western Roman Empire formally ended in 476 CE when the Germanic general Odoacer deposed the last emperor — just twenty-three years after Attila's death. The Huns didn't destroy Rome directly, but they set the Germanic migrations in motion that eventually did. The chain runs from Modu's steppe empire to Attila's European empire to the end of Rome.
As the Hunnic Empire dissolved, the Xianbei Northern Wei was unifying northern China — a 'barbarian' dynasty that would become more Chinese than the Chinese.
The grassland conveyor belt kept running: the Rouran held Mongolia, and the Gokturks were about to displace them — the same template Modu invented in 209 BCE, outliving every empire that used it.
